metadata.title: Patient-Reported Outcome Measures: Use in Medical Product Development to Support Labeling Claims — Guidance for Industry
metadata.deduplication_note: File named 15-physician-labeling-rule-format.pdf but document is the PRO guidance (December 2009), NOT the Physician Labeling Rule format guidance. The fda-labeling-be-rules.json in output-v4 covers PLR labeling rules (PLR-001 to PLR-055). This document is entirely distinct — it covers PRO instrument development and validation for labeling claims. Full extraction performed.

rules.rule_text: A PRO instrument (i.e., a questionnaire plus the information and documentation that supports its use) used to capture PRO data to measure treatment benefit or risk in medical product clinical trials must support claims in approved medical product labeling. The FDA will review the evidence that a particular PRO instrument measures the concept claimed.
rules.source_section: Section I — Introduction
rules.verbatim_quote: This guidance describes how the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) reviews and evaluates existing, modified, or newly created patient-reported outcome (PRO) instruments used to support claims in approved medical product labeling. A PRO instrument (i.e., a questionnaire plus the information and documentation that supports its use) is a means to capture PRO data used to measure treatment benefit or risk in medical product clinical trials.
rules.exceptions: Does not address use of PRO instruments for purposes beyond evaluation of claims made about a medical product in labeling

rules.rule_text: Findings measured by a well-defined and reliable PRO instrument in appropriately designed investigations can be used to support a claim in medical product labeling if the claim is consistent with the instrument's documented measurement capability. A PRO instrument should be shown to measure the concept it is intended to measure.
rules.source_section: Section II — Background
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, findings measured by a well-defined and reliable PRO instrument in appropriately designed investigations can be used to support a claim in medical product labeling if the claim is consistent with the instrument's documented measurement capability. The amount and kind of evidence that should be provided to the FDA is the same as for any other labeling claim based on other data. Use of a PRO instrument is advised when measuring a concept best known by the patient or best measured from the patient perspective. A PRO instrument, like physician-based instruments, should be shown to measure the concept it is intended to measure.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors should define the role a PRO endpoint is intended to play in the clinical trial (i.e., primary, key secondary, or exploratory endpoint) so that the instrument development and performance can be reviewed in the context of the intended role, and appropriate statistical methods can be planned and applied. This planning must be captured in an endpoint model.
rules.source_section: Section III.A — Endpoint Model
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should define the role a PRO endpoint is intended to play in the clinical trial (i.e., a primary, key secondary, or exploratory endpoint) so that the instrument development and performance can be reviewed in the context of the intended role, and appropriate statistical methods can be planned and applied. It is critical to plan these approaches in what can be called an endpoint model.

rules.rule_text: Early in medical product development, sponsors planning to use a PRO instrument in support of a labeling claim should determine whether an adequate PRO instrument exists to assess the concepts of interest. An instrument's measurement properties should be well established before enrollment begins for confirmatory clinical trials. Sponsors should begin instrument development and evaluation early in medical product development and engage the FDA in a discussion about a new or unique PRO instrument before confirmatory clinical trial protocols are finalized.
rules.source_section: Section III.B — Choice of PRO Instrument
rules.verbatim_quote: Early in medical product development, sponsors planning to use a PRO instrument in support of a labeling claim are encouraged to determine whether an adequate PRO instrument exists to assess and measure the concepts of interest... We suggest that an instrument's measurement properties be well established before enrollment begins for confirmatory clinical trials. Therefore, sponsors should begin instrument development and evaluation early in medical product development, and engage the FDA in a discussion about a new or unique PRO instrument before confirmatory clinical trial protocols are finalized.

rules.rule_text: The FDA will review documentation of PRO instrument development and testing in conjunction with clinical trial results to determine whether a labeling claim is substantiated. Sponsors should provide to the FDA: concepts being measured, number of items, conceptual framework, medical condition for intended use, population for intended use, data collection method, administration mode, response options, recall period, scoring, weighting of domains, format, respondent burden, and translation/cultural adaptation availability.
rules.source_section: Section III.B — Choice of PRO Instrument
rules.verbatim_quote: The FDA will review documentation of PRO instrument development and testing in conjunction with clinical trial results to determine whether a labeling claim is substantiated. The Appendix lists the type of PRO information sponsors should provide to the FDA to facilitate instrument review. Characteristics of PRO instruments that are reviewed by the FDA include the following: Concepts being measured... Number of items... Conceptual framework of the instrument... Medical condition for intended use... Population for intended use... Data collection method... Administration mode... Response options... Recall period... Scoring... Weighting of items or domains... Format... Respondent burden... Translation or cultural adaptation availability.

rules.rule_text: The adequacy of a proposed instrument to support a claim depends on the conceptual framework of the PRO instrument. The conceptual framework must explicitly define the concepts measured by the instrument in a diagram that presents a description of the relationships between items, domain (subconcepts), and concepts measured and the scores produced by a PRO instrument. The final framework is derived and confirmed by measurement property testing.
rules.source_section: Section III.C — Conceptual Framework of a PRO Instrument
rules.verbatim_quote: The adequacy of a proposed instrument to support a claim depends on the conceptual framework of the PRO instrument. The conceptual framework explicitly defines the concepts measured by the instrument in a diagram that presents a description of the relationships between items, domain (subconcepts), and concepts measured and the scores produced by a PRO instrument.

rules.rule_text: For instruments measuring general concepts (e.g., physical function), single-item PRO instruments do not provide a useful understanding of the treatment's effect because a stand-alone single item does not capture the domains of the general concept. Single-item questions about general concepts that include multiple items or domains rarely provide sufficient evidence to support claims about that general concept.
rules.source_section: Section III.C.1 — Concepts Measured
rules.verbatim_quote: If the concept of interest is general (e.g., physical function), a single-item PRO instrument does not provide a useful understanding of the treatment's effect because a stand-alone single item does not capture the domains of the general concept. For this reason, single-item questions about general concepts that include multiple items or domains rarely provide sufficient evidence to support claims about that general concept.
rules.exceptions: Single-item instruments are appropriate when a single item is a reliable and valid measure of the concept of interest (e.g., pain intensity)

rules.rule_text: Content validity is the extent to which the instrument measures the concept of interest. Content validity is supported by evidence from qualitative studies that the items and domains of an instrument are appropriate and comprehensive relative to its intended measurement concept, population, and use. Content validity is specific to the population, condition, and treatment to be studied. Evidence of other types of validity (e.g., construct validity) will not overcome problems with content validity.
rules.source_section: Section III.D — Content Validity
rules.verbatim_quote: Content validity is the extent to which the instrument measures the concept of interest. Content validity is supported by evidence from qualitative studies that the items and domains of an instrument are appropriate and comprehensive relative to its intended measurement concept, population, and use. Content validity is specific to the population, condition, and treatment to be studied... Evidence of other types of validity (e.g., construct validity) or reliability (e.g., consistent scores) will not overcome problems with content validity because we evaluate instrument adequacy to measure the concept represented by the labeling claim. It is important to establish content validity before other measurement properties are evaluated.

rules.rule_text: Item generation should include input from the target patient population to establish the items that reflect the concept of interest and contribute to its evaluation. The population will help generate item wording, evaluate completeness of item coverage, and perform initial assessment of clarity and readability. Item generation generally incorporates the input of a wide range of patients with the condition of interest to represent variations in severity and in population characteristics such as age, sex, ethnicity, and language groups in accordance with the anticipated clinical trial design.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.1 — Item Generation
rules.verbatim_quote: Item generation should include input from the target patient population to establish the items that reflect the concept of interest and contribute to its evaluation. The population will help generate item wording, evaluate the completeness of item coverage, and perform initial assessment of clarity and readability... Item generation generally incorporates the input of a wide range of patients with the condition of interest to represent variations in severity and in population characteristics such as age, sex, ethnicity, and language groups in accordance with the anticipated clinical trial design.

rules.rule_text: Without adequate documentation of patient input, a PRO instrument's content validity is likely to be questioned. FDA will review documentation to determine that the items cover all aspects of the concept important to patients, and that saturation has been reached. Saturation is reached at the point when no new relevant or important information emerges and collecting additional data will not likely add to the understanding of how patients perceive the concept of interest.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.1 — Item Generation
rules.verbatim_quote: Without adequate documentation of patient input, a PRO instrument's content validity is likely to be questioned. We will review documentation to determine that the items cover all aspects of the concept important to patients, and that saturation has been reached. Saturation is reached at the point when no new relevant or important information emerges and collecting additional data will not likely add to the understanding of how patients perceive the concept of interest and the items in the questionnaire.

rules.rule_text: Documentation provided to the FDA to support content validity should include all item generation techniques used, including any theoretical approach; the populations studied; source of items; selection, editing, and reduction of items; cognitive interview summaries or transcripts; pilot testing; importance ratings; and quantitative techniques for item evaluation.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.1 — Item Generation
rules.verbatim_quote: Documentation provided to the FDA to support content validity should include all item generation techniques used, including any theoretical approach; the populations studied; source of items; selection, editing, and reduction of items; cognitive interview summaries or transcripts; pilot testing; importance ratings; and quantitative techniques for item evaluation.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors should consider the data collection method and all procedures and protocols associated with the instrument administration mode. FDA will review data quality control procedures specific to the data collection method or instrument administration mode along with case report forms or screen shots of electronic PRO instruments. The PRO instrument format used in the clinical trial must be consistent with the format used during the instrument development process.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.2 — Data Collection Method and Instrument Administration Mode
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should consider the data collection method and all procedures and protocols associated with the instrument administration mode, including instructions to interviewers, instructions for self-administration, or instructions for supervising self-administration. We will review data quality control procedures specific to the data collection method or instrument administration mode along with case report forms or screen shots of electronic PRO instruments... It is important that the PRO instrument format used in the clinical trial be consistent with the format that is used during the instrument development process.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors should evaluate the rationale and appropriateness of the recall period for a PRO instrument. PRO instruments that call for patients to rely on memory, especially if they must recall over a long period of time, compare their current state with an earlier period, or average their response over a period of time, are likely to undermine content validity. Items with short recall periods or items that ask patients to describe their current or recent state are usually preferable.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.3 — Recall Period
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should also evaluate the rationale and the appropriateness of the recall period for a PRO instrument... PRO instruments that call for patients to rely on memory, especially if they must recall over a long period of time, compare their current state with an earlier period, or average their response over a period of time, are likely to undermine content validity. Response is likely to be influenced by the patient's state at the time of recall. For these reasons, items with short recall periods or items that ask patients to describe their current or recent state are usually preferable.
rules.exceptions: Detailed recall may be necessary in some cases; if so, use appropriate methods such as diary-based collection

rules.rule_text: Items that ask patients to respond hypothetically (e.g., whether they perceived they could perform daily activities) are not recommended for clinical trials. Multi-item instruments should have all items relevant to most of the patients in the clinical trial. Use of not applicable response options is not useful and creates problems with scoring. Skip patterns may create difficulties in administration.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.1 — Item Generation
rules.verbatim_quote: Items that ask patients to respond hypothetically may cause patients to respond on the basis of their desired condition rather than on their actual condition and therefore are not recommended for clinical trials... When using multi-item instruments, it is important that all items be relevant to most of the patients in the clinical trial... Use of not applicable response options is not useful. Skip patterns may create difficulties in administration.

rules.rule_text: Item response options are considered appropriate when: wording used in responses is clear and appropriate; response options are appropriate for the intended population; responses offer a clear distinction between choices; instructions to patients for completing items are adequate; the number of response options is justified empirically; responses for an item are appropriately ordered and represent similar intervals; responses avoid potential ceiling or floor effects; and responses do not bias the direction of responses.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.4 — Response Options
rules.verbatim_quote: Item response options generally are considered appropriate when: Wording used in responses is clear and appropriate... The item response options are appropriate for the intended population... Responses offer a clear distinction between choices... Instructions to patients for completing items and selecting responses for the items are adequate... The number of response options is justified empirically... Responses for an item are appropriately ordered and represent similar intervals... Responses for items avoid potential ceiling or floor effects... Responses do not bias the direction of responses.

rules.rule_text: The user manual provided by a developer during the PRO instrument development process should specify how to incorporate the instrument into a clinical trial in a way that minimizes administrator burden, patient burden, missing data, and poor data quality. The user manual should explain to investigators and interviewers critical principles of PRO administration.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.5 — Instrument Format, Instructions, and Training
rules.verbatim_quote: We recommend that the user manual provided by a developer during the PRO instrument development process specify how to incorporate the instrument into a clinical trial in a way that minimizes administrator burden, patient burden, missing data, and poor data quality. The user manual should explain to investigators and interviewers critical principles of PRO administration.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must examine all items and procedures in a pilot test of whether patients understand the items and instructions included in the PRO instrument. This examination should include documentation that the concepts in the PRO instrument's conceptual framework are confirmed, that the response options and recall period are appropriately comprehended, and that the instrument's readability is adequate for the intended population. Evidence from cognitive interview studies can be used to determine when a concept is adequately captured.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.6 — Patient Understanding
rules.verbatim_quote: When the initial and subsequent drafts of an instrument are prepared, sponsors are encouraged to examine all items and procedures in a pilot test of whether patients understand the items and instructions included in the PRO instrument. This examination should include documentation that the concepts represented in the PRO instrument's conceptual framework are confirmed, that the response options and recall period are appropriately comprehended, and that the instrument's readability is adequate for the intended population.

rules.rule_text: Once content validity has been established, FDA intends to consider the following additional measurement properties during PRO instrument review: reliability, construct validity, and ability to detect change. FDA will review these measurement properties in light of the clinical trial's objectives, design, enrolled population, and statistical analysis plan (SAP).
rules.source_section: Section III.E — Reliability, Other Validity, and Ability to Detect Change
rules.verbatim_quote: Once the instrument's content validity has been established, we intend to consider the following additional measurement properties during FDA review of a PRO instrument: reliability, construct validity, and ability to detect change. We plan to review the measurement properties that are specific to the documented PRO instrument's conceptual framework, confirmed scoring algorithm, administration procedures, and questionnaire format in light of the clinical trial's objectives, design, enrolled population, and statistical analysis plan (SAP).

rules.rule_text: Test-retest reliability must be demonstrated. For interviewer-administered PROs, inter-interviewer reliability (measured by interclass correlation coefficient) must also be demonstrated. Internal consistency reliability tests (e.g., Cronbach's alpha) alone may not constitute sufficient evidence of reliability for clinical trial purposes.
rules.source_section: Section III.E.1 — Reliability
rules.verbatim_quote: We will review documentation of tests to determine if reproducibility (e.g., test-retest reliability) has been demonstrated... Internal consistency reliability tests (e.g., Cronbach's alpha) to determine agreement among responses to different questions, in the absence of test-retest reliability, may not constitute sufficient evidence of reliability for clinical trial purposes.
rules.exceptions: Test-retest reliability may be difficult or impossible to establish for remitting and relapsing or episodic diseases

rules.rule_text: FDA will review construct validity evidence including discriminant and convergent validity (strength of correlation testing a priori hypotheses) and known groups validity (degree to which the PRO instrument can distinguish among groups hypothesized a priori to be different). FDA will also review ability to detect change evidence including within person change over time and effect size statistics.
rules.source_section: Section III.E.2 — Other Validity / Section III.E.3 — Ability to Detect Change
rules.verbatim_quote: Construct validity is determined by evidence that relationships among items, domains, and concepts conform to a priori hypotheses concerning logical relationships that should exist with other measures or characteristics of patients and patient groups... We will also review evidence that the instrument can differentiate between clinically distinct groups (i.e., known groups validity).

rules.rule_text: When a PRO instrument is modified, sponsors generally should provide evidence to confirm the new instrument's adequacy. Changes that can alter the way patients respond to the same set of questions include: changing an instrument from paper to electronic format; changing administration timing; changing the application to a different setting, population, or condition; changing the order of items, item wording, response options, or recall period; or changing the instructions.
rules.source_section: Section III.F — Instrument Modification
rules.verbatim_quote: When a PRO instrument is modified, sponsors generally should provide evidence to confirm the new instrument's adequacy... Examples of changes that can alter the way that patients respond to the same set of questions include: Changing an instrument from paper to electronic format... Changing the timing of or procedures for PRO instrument administration within the clinic visit... Changing the application to a different setting, population, or condition... Changing the order of items, item wording, response options, or recall period or deleting portions of a questionnaire... Changing the instructions or the placement of instructions within the PRO instrument.
rules.exceptions: Not every small change necessitates extensive studies; additional qualitative work may be adequate depending on the type of modification

rules.rule_text: For PRO instruments intended for use with children and adolescents, FDA discourages proxy-reported outcome measures. Instrument development within fairly narrow age groupings is important to account for developmental differences. For patients who cannot respond for themselves (e.g., infants), FDA encourages observer reports that include only those events or behaviors that can be observed.
rules.source_section: Section III.G.1 — Children and Adolescents
rules.verbatim_quote: We discourage proxy-reported outcome measures for this population (i.e., reports by someone who is not the patient responding as if that person were the patient). For patients who cannot respond for themselves (e.g., infant patients), we encourage observer reports that include only those events or behaviors that can be observed.
rules.exceptions: Observer reports are acceptable for events/behaviors that can be objectively observed

rules.rule_text: Because many development programs are multinational, when PRO instruments are applied to multiple cultures or languages, sponsors must provide evidence that the content validity and other measurement properties are adequately similar between all versions used in the clinical trial. FDA will review the process used to translate and culturally adapt the instrument for populations that will use them in the trial.
rules.source_section: Section III.G.3 — Culture or Language Subgroups
rules.verbatim_quote: Regardless of whether the instrument was developed concurrently in multiple cultures or languages or whether a fully developed instrument was adapted or translated to new cultures or languages, we recommend that sponsors provide evidence that the content validity and other measurement properties are adequately similar between all versions used in the clinical trial. We will review the process used to translate and culturally adapt the instrument for populations that will use them in the trial.

rules.rule_text: If the PRO measurement goal is to support labeling claims, PRO concept measurement should be stated as a specific clinical trial objective or hypothesis. It is critical that the clinical trial protocol define the endpoint measures and the criteria for the statistical analysis and interpretation of results before data are unblinded; determination of these criteria after data are unblinded will not be credible. Sponsors should avoid post-hoc selective picking of PRO endpoint results for inclusion in proposed labeling.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D — Design Considerations for Multiple Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: If the PRO measurement goal is to support labeling claims, PRO concept measurement should be stated as a specific clinical trial objective or hypothesis... It is critical that the clinical trial protocol define the endpoint measures and the criteria for the statistical analysis and interpretation of results, including a specification of the conditions for a positive clinical trial conclusion, because determination of these criteria and conditions after data are unblinded will not be credible. Sponsors should avoid separate consideration of PRO endpoints from the clinical trial's primary objectives in terms of clinical trial design or data analysis. Sponsors also should avoid cherry picking or post hoc selective picking of PRO endpoint results for inclusion in proposed labeling.

rules.rule_text: Open-label clinical trials are rarely adequate to support labeling claims based on PRO instruments because patients who know they are in an active treatment group may overestimate benefit. PRO instruments administered during a clinic visit should be administered before other clinical assessments or procedures to prevent influencing patient perceptions.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A.1 — Blinding and Randomization
rules.verbatim_quote: Open-label clinical trials, where patients and investigators are aware of assigned therapy, are rarely adequate to support labeling claims based on PRO instruments. Patients who know they are in an active treatment group may overestimate benefit whereas patients who know they are not receiving active treatment may underreport any improvement actually experienced. For the same reasons, to prevent influencing patient perceptions, PRO instruments administered during a clinic visit should be administered before other clinical assessments or procedures.
rules.exceptions: Some medical devices or treatment regimens cannot be blinded; in those cases sponsors should confer with the appropriate review division

rules.rule_text: For electronic PRO instruments, sponsors must ensure that FDA regulatory requirements are met for sponsor and investigator record keeping, maintenance, and access. The clinical trial protocol, or a separate document, should specify how the electronic PRO source data will be maintained and how the investigator will meet the regulatory requirements. Electronic PRO data should be consistent with 21 CFR part 11 data standards.
rules.source_section: Section IV.F — Specific Concerns When Using Electronic PRO Instruments
rules.verbatim_quote: When PRO instruments are used, sponsors must ensure that FDA regulatory requirements are met for sponsor and investigator record keeping, maintenance, and access. These responsibilities are independent of the method used to record clinical trial data and, therefore, apply to all types of PRO data including electronic PRO data... The clinical trial protocol, or a separate document, should specify how the electronic PRO source data will be maintained and how the investigator will meet the regulatory requirements.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must avoid the following with electronic PRO data: direct data transmission from the PRO device to the sponsor without an electronic audit trail; source document control by the sponsor exclusively; inability of clinical investigator to maintain and confirm electronic PRO data accuracy; existence of only one database without backup; ability of any entity other than the investigator to modify source data; loss of adverse event data; premature or unplanned access to unblinded data; and direct PRO data transmission of safety information without ensuring timely transmission to the clinical investigator.
rules.source_section: Section IV.F — Specific Concerns When Using Electronic PRO Instruments
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors also should avoid the following: Direct PRO data transmission from the PRO data collection device to the sponsor, clinical investigator, or other third party without an electronic audit trail that documents all changes to the data after it leaves the PRO data collection device. Source document control by the sponsor exclusively. Clinical investigator inability to maintain and confirm electronic PRO data accuracy... The existence of only one database without backup... Ability of any entity other than the investigator (and/or site staff designated by the investigator) to modify the source data. Loss of adverse event data. Premature or unplanned access to unblinded data.

rules.rule_text: The clinical trial protocol should describe how missing data will be handled in the analysis. The protocol should include backup plans for gathering all treatment-related reasons for patients failing to report at scheduled times or withdrawing from a treatment or clinical trial. The statistical analysis plan (SAP) should address plans for how the statistical analyses will handle missing data. Sponsors are recommended to propose two or more sensitivity analyses with different methods for missing data imputation.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A.3 — Handling Missing Data
rules.verbatim_quote: The clinical trial protocol should describe how missing data will be handled in the analysis... The protocol can increase the likelihood that a clinical trial will still be informative by establishing backup plans for gathering all treatment-related reasons for patients failing to report at scheduled times or withdrawing from a treatment or the clinical trial... We recommend that in the protocol the sponsor propose two or more sensitivity analyses with different methods for missing data imputation.

rules.rule_text: The statistical analysis plan (SAP) should describe the planned primary analysis in detail noting whether the endpoint will be analyzed as a continuous variable, dichotomous variable, or graded response; the primary and secondary endpoints; adjustments for multiplicity to control the overall Type I error rate; and the specific statistical methods planned. Sponsors should provide the FDA with the clinical trial's SAP for review.
rules.source_section: Section V.B — Statistical Considerations for Using Multiple Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: The SAP should describe the planned primary analysis in detail noting whether the endpoint will be analyzed as a continuous variable (mean scores), dichotomous variable (success or failure), or some graded response; the primary and secondary endpoints; adjustments for multiplicity to control the overall Type I error rate; and the specific statistical methods planned. Sponsors should provide the FDA with the clinical trial's SAP for review.

rules.rule_text: FDA discourages proposing labeling claims based on statistical significance alone, because statistical significance can sometimes be achieved for small changes in PRO measures that may not be clinically meaningful. To demonstrate treatment benefit, FDA finds it informative to examine the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of responses between treatment groups to characterize the treatment effect.
rules.source_section: Section V.E — Interpretation of Clinical Trial Results
rules.verbatim_quote: Because statistical significance can sometimes be achieved for small changes in PRO measures that may not be clinically meaningful (i.e., do not indicate treatment benefit), we encourage sponsors to avoid proposing labeling claims based on statistical significance alone. To demonstrate treatment benefit, we find it informative to examine the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of responses between treatment groups to characterize the treatment effect.

rules.rule_text: If PRO information is provided electronically for FDA review, it should be placed in section 5.3.5.3 of the electronic common technical document (eCTD). The submission must include the exact version of the instrument proposed or used in the clinical trial under review and all instructions for use, including screen shots or interviewer scripts, if relevant.
rules.source_section: Appendix — Information on a PRO Instrument Reviewed by the FDA
rules.verbatim_quote: If the PRO information is provided electronically, it should be placed in section 5.3.5.3 of the electronic common technical document... Exact version of the instrument proposed or used in the clinical trial (protocol) under review and all instructions for use. Include screen shots or interviewer scripts, if relevant.

rules.rule_text: Content validity documentation submitted to FDA must include evidence that instrument captures all of the most clinically important concepts and items, including: literature review and documentation of expert input; qualitative study protocols, interview guides, and summary of results for focus group testing, open-ended patient interviews, and cognitive interviews; origin and derivation of items with chronology; item tracking matrix showing items retained and deleted providing evidence of saturation; and qualitative study summary supporting content validity for item content, response options, recall period, and scoring.
rules.source_section: Appendix — Section V: Content Validity Documentation
rules.verbatim_quote: Evidence that instrument captures all of the most clinically important concepts and items, and that items are complete, relevant (appropriate), and understandable to the patient... A. Literature review and documentation of expert input B. Qualitative study protocols, interview guides, and summary of results for: 1. Focus group testing 2. Open-ended patient interviews 3. Cognitive interviews C. Origin and derivation of items with chronology of events for item generation, modification, and finalization. Item tracking matrix for versions tested with patients showing items retained and items deleted providing evidence of saturation.

rules.rule_text: PRO instruments that measure safety concerns (symptoms or signs that are best captured from the patient perspective) must follow the same PRO instrument development principles. As with any clinical trial evaluating FDA-regulated medical products, all adverse events detected with a PRO instrument should be included in the clinical trial report.
rules.source_section: Section III.B — Choice of PRO Instrument
rules.verbatim_quote: PRO instruments can be used to measure important safety concerns if those concerns represent symptoms or signs that are best captured from the patient perspective. The principles for PRO instrument development are not different for this application... As with any clinical trial evaluating FDA-regulated medical products, all adverse events detected with a PRO instrument should be included in the clinical trial report.

rules.rule_text: A multidomain PRO measure may successfully support a labeling claim based on one or a subset of the domains measured if an a priori analysis plan prespecifies the domains that will be targeted as endpoints and the method of analysis that will adjust for the multiplicity of tests for the specific claim. The use of domain subsets as clinical trial endpoints presupposes that the PRO instrument was adequately developed and validated to measure the subset of domains independently.
rules.source_section: Section V.B — Statistical Considerations for Using Multiple Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: A multidomain PRO measure may successfully support a labeling claim based on one or a subset of the domains measured if an a priori analysis plan prespecifies the domains that will be targeted as endpoints and the method of analysis that will adjust for the multiplicity of tests for the specific claim. The use of domain subsets as clinical trial endpoints presupposes that the PRO instrument was adequately developed and validated to measure the subset of domains independently from the other domains.

rules.rule_text: For composite PRO endpoints, FDA discourages their use for confirmatory clinical trials when large variations are predicted to exist between its components. Individual components of a composite endpoint will generally not be adequate to support a labeling claim for those components unless the components are prespecified in the protocol as separate endpoints and all prespecified components are reported in labeling.
rules.source_section: Section V.C — Statistical Considerations for Composite Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: Therefore, we discourage the use of a composite endpoint for confirmatory clinical trials when large variations are predicted to exist between its components. Multiplicity problems arise when the multiple individual components of a composite endpoint are intended as possible claims. In general, individual components of a composite endpoint will not be adequate to support a labeling claim for the components unless the components are prespecified in the protocol as separate endpoints and all prespecified components are reported in labeling.
rules.exceptions: Composite endpoints can reduce multiplicity problems when components are of similar importance, similar frequency, and similar treatment effects
